requests.name: Seed — Set company to TRIAL with past trial_ends_at
requests.docs:
STEP 1 — Get your company ID. Then run this SQL directly in your DB to set an expired trial:

UPDATE "Company"
SET subscription_status = 'TRIAL', trial_ends_at = '2020-01-01T00:00:00Z'
WHERE id = '<your-company-id>';

This simulates an expired trial. Sign back in after running the SQL.

requests.name: Trigger — POST /internal/trials/expire-now
requests.docs: Manually triggers the trial expiry logic. Only works when NODE_ENV != 'production'. Returns { locked: N } where N is the number of companies that were just locked. If you seeded one expired trial in Setup, expect locked=1.

requests.name: Verify — company is now LOCKED
requests.docs: After running the manual trigger, the seeded company should now have subscription_status=LOCKED. GET is allowed even for LOCKED companies (SubscriptionGuard only blocks writes).

requests.name: Verify — LOCKED company write blocked → 403
requests.docs: Confirms that a newly-LOCKED company is immediately blocked from write operations by SubscriptionGuard.

requests.name: Verify — trigger again is idempotent → locked=0
requests.docs: Run the trigger a second time. The company is already LOCKED so Prisma's WHERE clause (subscription_status=TRIAL) excludes it. Expect locked=0. Confirms the cron is idempotent.

requests.name: Verify — ACTIVE company with past trial_ends_at stays ACTIVE
Verify — TRIAL with null trial_ends_at stays TRIAL
Verify — TRIAL with future trial_ends_at stays TRIAL
Verify — endpoint blocked in production → 403
requests.docs: Only run this against a production or production-like deployment where NODE_ENV=production. The endpoint must return 403 and never execute the expiry logic.
